$schema_notes: Data curated for hackathon prototype. Every DOI and PMID below was surfaced from primary-source searches (Nature, eLife, Cell Reports, PNAS, Annual Reviews, etc.). Before shipping, open each DOI once to confirm it resolves — 6-hour deadlines make one broken link the thing judges notice.

longevity.label: Extreme Longevity
longevity.description: Maximum lifespan far exceeding what body size predicts. Relevant to geroscience and healthspan extension.

cancer_resistance.label: Cancer Resistance
cancer_resistance.description: Very low spontaneous tumor incidence, often despite large body size or long lifespan (Peto's paradox resolvers).

dna_repair.label: Enhanced DNA Repair
dna_repair.description: Elevated double-strand break repair fidelity, nucleotide excision repair, or mismatch repair relative to short-lived mammals.

viral_tolerance.label: Viral Tolerance
viral_tolerance.description: Asymptomatic carriage of viruses that cause severe disease in other mammals, typically via dampened inflammation rather than sterilizing immunity.

innate_immunity.label: Tuned Innate Immunity
innate_immunity.description: Constitutive interferon expression, altered inflammasome activity, or unique pathogen sensing — relevant to autoimmunity, cytokine storm, and inflammaging.

hypoxia_tolerance.label: Hypoxia / Ischemia Tolerance
hypoxia_tolerance.description: Brain and tissue resilience to low oxygen; relevant to stroke, cardiac arrest, and organ transplantation.

hibernation.label: Hibernation & Metabolic Suppression
hibernation.description: Profound reversible metabolic depression without organ damage. Relevant to neuroprotection, trauma medicine, and space travel.

regeneration.label: Tissue Regeneration
regeneration.description: Mammalian regeneration of structures normally lost to scar tissue in humans — ear pinna, spinal cord, digit tips, antler.

Heterocephalus glaber.headline: Lives >37 years with near-zero cancer incidence and survives 18 minutes of total oxygen deprivation.

longevity.summary: Naked mole-rats (NMRs) have the longest maximum lifespan of any rodent, exceeding 37 years — roughly ten times that of a similarly-sized mouse. Their mortality hazard does not rise with age, violating the Gompertz law that governs aging in most mammals. Proposed mechanisms include high-molecular-mass hyaluronan (HMM-HA) produced by fibroblasts, unusually stable proteostasis, robust DNA repair, and a chromatin state resistant to Yamanaka-factor reprogramming. The NMR is now a staple model for geroscience and interventional aging research.

citations.label: Ruby et al. 2018, eLife — NMR mortality defies Gompertz
Tian et al. 2013, Nature — HMM-HA mediates cancer resistance
Lewis et al. 2015, PNAS — Nrf2 signaling and longevity
Kim et al. 2011, Nature — NMR genome sequencing

cancer_resistance.summary: NMRs display extreme resistance to spontaneous tumors and resist experimental carcinogenesis. The dominant mechanism described to date is HMM-HA, which interacts with CD44 to trigger early contact inhibition via p16INK4a. NMR cells also engage a secondary 'pALT' isoform from the INK4 locus and show unusual hypersensitivity to loss of single tumor suppressors, which triggers apoptosis rather than proliferation. Cagan et al. additionally showed NMRs are an outlier in the somatic mutation rate vs body-mass relationship, accumulating mutations slowly.

citations.label: Tian et al. 2013, Nature — HMM-HA cancer resistance
Seluanov et al. 2018, Nat Rev Cancer — Cancer resistance in long-lived mammals
Cagan et al. 2022, Nature — Somatic mutation rates scale with lifespan

hypoxia_tolerance.summary: NMRs live in crowded underground burrows with oxygen levels that would be lethal to most mammals. Park et al. showed NMRs switch to fructose-driven anaerobic glycolysis under anoxia, allowing brain tissue to survive 18 minutes of zero-oxygen exposure — a feat no other mammal matches. Their GluN2D NMDA-receptor subunit persists into adulthood, delaying calcium-driven excitotoxicity during hypoxic stress.

citations.label: Park et al. 2017, Science — Fructose-driven anoxia survival in NMRs
Omotoso et al. 2021, Biogerontology — NMR hypoxia mechanisms review

Balaena mysticetus.headline: Longest-lived mammal (>200 years) with enhanced DNA double-strand break repair via elevated CIRBP.

longevity.summary: Bowheads are estimated to live over 200 years — recovery of stone harpoon points from living individuals corroborates this. Keane et al. sequenced the bowhead genome and identified bowhead-specific substitutions in ERCC1 (nucleotide excision repair) and duplications of PCNA and LAMTOR1. A 2025 Nature paper from the Gorbunova/Seluanov lab identified cold-inducible RNA-binding protein (CIRBP) as a major driver of their enhanced DNA repair — overexpressing bowhead CIRBP in Drosophila extended lifespan and increased resistance to ionizing radiation.

citations.label: Keane et al. 2015, Cell Reports — Bowhead genome & longevity
Firsanov et al. 2025, Nature — CIRBP drives DNA repair in bowhead whale

dna_repair.summary: Bowhead fibroblasts exhibit enhanced double-strand break repair capacity and fidelity compared to human, mouse, and cow cells, along with lower mutation rates. CIRBP is expressed at very high levels in bowhead tissues and, when overexpressed in human and Drosophila cells, improves DNA damage resistance. Paradoxically, bowhead cells require fewer oncogenic hits than human cells to transform — suggesting their cancer resistance is front-loaded at the mutation-prevention step rather than at transformation.

citations.label: Firsanov et al. 2025, Nature — CIRBP DNA repair in bowhead
Keane et al. 2015, Cell Reports — ERCC1, PCNA

cancer_resistance.summary: Despite being the second-largest animal on Earth (>80,000 kg) and the longest-lived mammal, bowheads show very low cancer incidence — a canonical example of Peto's paradox. Unlike elephants, they lack TP53 duplications. The mechanism instead appears to be radically enhanced genome maintenance (low mutation accumulation) combined with a CDKN2C duplication that reinforces cell cycle checkpoints.

citations.label: Toren et al. 2024, Heliyon — DNA repair & anti-cancer in bowhead
Seluanov et al. 2018, Nat Rev Cancer — Review

Loxodonta africana.headline: Carries 20 copies of TP53 and a resurrected LIF6 'zombie' gene that trigger apoptosis on DNA damage.

cancer_resistance.summary: Elephants are the textbook Peto's-paradox species: 100× more cells than humans but cancer mortality of only ~3% vs 11-25% in humans. Abegglen et al. (JAMA 2015) and Sulak et al. (eLife 2016) independently showed elephants carry ~20 copies of TP53 (1 canonical + 19 retrogenes) that arose via segmental duplication as proboscideans evolved large body size ~40 Mya. Vazquez et al. (Cell Reports 2018) showed LIF6 — a pseudogene resurrected in elephants — is transcribed by TP53 on DNA damage and localizes to mitochondria to trigger apoptosis. Elephant peripheral blood lymphocytes undergo apoptosis at 2-3× the rate of human controls after genotoxic stress.

citations.label: Abegglen et al. 2015, JAMA — 20 copies of TP53
Sulak et al. 2016, eLife — TP53 copy number expansion & body size
Vazquez et al. 2018, Cell Reports — LIF6 zombie gene

longevity.summary: Elephants live 60-70 years in the wild, extraordinary for such a large terrestrial mammal. Their TP53 amplification and resulting DNA damage hypersensitivity are believed to have co-evolved with body size — large, long-lived animals need proportionally stronger tumor suppression to maintain lifespan. Additional proposed mechanisms include telomere dynamics and reduced telomerase activity as a cancer-prevention trade-off.

citations.label: Caulin & Maley 2011, Trends Ecol Evol — Peto's paradox
Abegglen et al. 2015, JAMA

Elephas maximus.headline: Independent confirmation of 20× TP53 copy expansion — shares cancer-resistance architecture with African elephant.

cancer_resistance.summary: Both Elephas maximus and Loxodonta africana genomes contain multiple copies of TP53 retrogenes, consistent with an ancestral proboscidean expansion. Asian elephant cells likewise show DNA-damage hypersensitivity and elevated apoptosis, offering a second independent line of evidence that TP53-pathway dosage underlies elephant cancer resistance.

citations.label: Abegglen et al. 2015, JAMA
Sulak et al. 2016, eLife

Pteropus alecto.headline: Constitutive interferon-α expression plus dampened NLRP3 inflammasome — hosts Hendra, Nipah, and filoviruses without symptoms.

viral_tolerance.summary: Pteropus alecto is a natural reservoir for Hendra virus, Nipah virus, and henipaviruses without apparent disease. Zhou et al. (PNAS 2016) showed P. alecto has a contracted type-I IFN locus but constitutively expresses IFN-α in healthy tissue — a tonic antiviral state. Ahn et al. (Nat Microbiol 2019) demonstrated NLRP3-mediated inflammation is profoundly dampened by three mechanisms: reduced transcriptional priming, a unique splice variant, and a modified leucine-rich repeat domain. Ahn et al. (Cell 2023) identified ASC2 (PYDC1), previously thought primate-specific, as a bat inflammasome suppressor that blocks ASC speck formation.

citations.label: Zhou et al. 2016, PNAS — Constitutive IFN-α in Pteropus
Ahn et al. 2019, Nat Microbiol — Dampened NLRP3
Ahn et al. 2023, Cell — Bat ASC2 suppresses inflammasomes

innate_immunity.summary: Bat innate immunity is characterized by 'tolerance' rather than 'resistance': high antiviral readiness (constitutive ISGs, elevated IRF7, expanded IFN-ω locus in some species) combined with muted inflammation (PYHIN gene family loss, ASC2 expression, altered c-Rel binding in the TNFα promoter). This de-coupling is attractive for human therapy because cytokine storm — not viral load — drives much of the morbidity in COVID-19, sepsis, and autoinflammatory disease.

citations.label: Ahn et al. 2016, Sci Rep — PYHIN loss in bats
Ahn et al. 2023, Cell — ASC2
Irving et al. 2021, Nature — Lessons from the host defences of bats

longevity.summary: Despite high metabolic rates from flight, many bats live far longer than predicted by body mass. Longevity correlates with the same dampened inflammatory pathways implicated in viral tolerance — inflammaging is reduced. Brandt's bat (Myotis brandtii) is the record-holder.

citations.label: Foley et al. 2018, Sci Adv — Bat longevity genomics

Myotis brandtii.headline: 7-gram bat that lives 41+ years — the most extreme body-mass-to-lifespan ratio of any mammal.

longevity.summary: Brandt's bat holds the record for longest lifespan relative to body mass in mammals — a 7g animal that can live over 41 years. Seim et al. (Nature Commun 2013) sequenced its genome and identified mutations in GHR and IGF1R that alter growth hormone signaling — the same pathways implicated in long-lived Ames and Snell dwarf mice. Combined with hibernation (which reduces annual metabolic load) and the general bat dampened-inflammation profile, GH/IGF-1 reduction appears to drive this outlier longevity.

citations.label: Seim et al. 2013, Nature Commun — Myotis brandtii genome

viral_tolerance.summary: As a vespertilionid bat, Myotis brandtii shares the broad chiropteran innate-immunity profile: dampened NLRP3, PYHIN family loss, and elevated baseline IFN signaling. These traits are thought to have co-evolved with flight — elevated self-DNA release from high metabolic activity required suppression of DNA-triggered inflammation.
human_targets: Same as Pteropus alecto viral-tolerance pathways

citations.label: Ahn et al. 2019, Nat Microbiol

Desmodus rotundus.headline: Saliva anticoagulant 'desmoteplase' (DSPA) was in Phase III trials for ischemic stroke.

innate_immunity.summary: Vampire bat saliva contains desmoteplase (DSPA α1), a plasminogen activator with much higher fibrin-specificity than human tPA. It reached Phase III clinical trials (DIAS-3, DIAS-4) for extending the ischemic stroke treatment window beyond 4.5 hours. While the Phase III trials did not meet primary endpoints, DSPA remains a case study in bat-derived therapeutics and fibrin-selective thrombolysis.

citations.label: Albers et al. 2015, Lancet Neurol — DIAS-3 trial
Reddrop et al. 2005, Stroke — DSPA vs tPA

viral_tolerance.summary: Vampire bats are significant rabies reservoirs and carry SARS-related coronaviruses. Like other bats, their innate immunity is tuned toward tolerance. Zepeda Mendoza et al. (Nature Ecol Evol 2018) sequenced the D. rotundus genome and gut metagenome, finding immune adaptations alongside the unique blood-feeding phenotype.

citations.label: Zepeda Mendoza et al. 2018, Nat Ecol Evol — Vampire bat hologenome

Ictidomys tridecemlineatus.headline: Cerebral blood flow drops 90% during torpor with no brain damage — a natural model for stroke neuroprotection.

hibernation.summary: I. tridecemlineatus is the premier hibernation model organism. During torpor, body temperature falls to 2-10°C, heart rate drops below 5% of normal, and cerebral blood flow is reduced by 90% — conditions that would be fatal-level ischemia in humans. Yet the squirrels emerge each spring without detectable brain damage. Tau protein is reversibly hyperphosphorylated during torpor (a state resembling Alzheimer pathology that fully resolves on arousal), suggesting tau aggregation is not a one-way street. Melatonin-receptor signaling, STAT3 activation, and cytoskeletal remodeling have all been implicated in the neuroprotective phenotype.

citations.label: Frerichs et al. 1994, JCBFM — 90% CBF reduction without damage
Schwartz et al. 2013, PLOS ONE — Seasonal brain gene expression
Sahdo et al. 2013, PLOS ONE — Hibernation proteomics
Arendt et al. 2003, J Neurosci — Reversible tau hyperphosphorylation

hypoxia_tolerance.summary: Ground squirrel tissues and isolated mitochondria tolerate anoxia followed by rapid reoxygenation in vitro. A 2025 JEB study showed that arousal from hibernation dramatically increases blood oxygen saturation without the reperfusion injury seen in human stroke. The ischemia-reperfusion tolerance phenotype is an active target for drug discovery aimed at stroke, cardiac arrest, and organ preservation.

citations.label: Duffy et al. 2025, J Exp Biol — Arousal and blood O2 saturation
Dave et al. 2012, Comp Biochem Physiol B — Neuroprotection from hibernators

Nannospalax galili.image_hint: blind mole rat Spalax Nannospalax galili
Nannospalax galili.headline: Secretes IFN-β to trigger 'concerted cell death' in pre-malignant tissue; cancer has never been observed in the wild.

cancer_resistance.summary: Blind mole-rats (Spalax / Nannospalax) live ~20 years, ten times the mouse lifespan, and have never been observed with spontaneous tumors. Gorbunova et al. (PNAS 2012) discovered that when Spalax fibroblasts hyperproliferate, they release interferon-β to trigger concerted necrotic cell death of the entire population — an extreme fail-safe that eliminates pre-malignant clones. Zhao et al. (Nat Immunol 2021) additionally showed retrotransposon reactivation triggers a cGAS–STING innate immune response that confers cancer resistance.

citations.label: Gorbunova et al. 2012, PNAS — Concerted necrotic cell death
Zhao et al. 2021, Nat Immunol — Transposon-triggered innate immunity

hypoxia_tolerance.summary: Spalax lives in sealed subterranean tunnels at ~7% oxygen — below the level at which most mammals develop pulmonary edema. Their VEGF expression is elevated, HIF-1α regulation is altered, and a unique p53 variant with a lysine-to-arginine substitution at position 174 shifts its response from apoptosis toward cell cycle arrest under hypoxia.

citations.label: Ashur-Fabian et al. 2004, PNAS — Evolution of p53 in hypoxia-adapted Spalax

Camelus dromedarius.headline: Heavy-chain-only antibodies (nanobodies) — now a clinical therapeutic platform (caplacizumab).

innate_immunity.summary: Camelids (camels, llamas, alpacas) produce a unique class of heavy-chain-only antibodies (HCAbs) lacking light chains. Their variable domain — the VHH or 'nanobody' — is 15 kDa (vs 150 kDa for IgG), highly stable, and can reach epitopes conventional antibodies cannot. Caplacizumab (Cablivi), a nanobody targeting vWF, was approved by the FDA in 2019 for acquired thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura. Nanobody platforms are now in trials for oncology, COVID-19, and neurodegeneration.

citations.label: Hamers-Casterman et al. 1993, Nature — Heavy-chain-only antibodies
Scully et al. 2019, NEJM — Caplacizumab for TTP

Leptonychotes weddellii.headline: Dives to 600m for 80 minutes on a single breath — extreme tolerance to hypoxia and CO₂.

hypoxia_tolerance.summary: Weddell seals make the deepest and longest dives of any pinniped. They tolerate arterial PO₂ levels that would cause unconsciousness in humans, aided by massive myoglobin stores in skeletal muscle, bradycardia during the dive response, and peripheral vasoconstriction that shunts blood to brain and heart. Mirceta et al. (Science 2013) found that deep-diving mammals convergently evolved high net surface charge on myoglobin, preventing aggregation at extreme concentrations. Seal cardiovascular physiology informs organ-preservation and cardiac-arrest research.

citations.label: Mirceta et al. 2013, Science — Myoglobin charge and dive capacity
Allen & Vázquez-Medina 2019, Front Physiol — Hypoxia tolerance in marine mammals

Ursus maritimus.headline: Pregnant females fast for 8 months in dens without muscle loss, osteoporosis, or kidney failure.

hibernation.summary: Bears are 'carnivoran hibernators' — unlike rodents, their body temperature drops only ~5-6°C while metabolism falls ~75%. They maintain muscle and bone mass during multi-month fasts, recycle urea nitrogen into protein, and emerge insulin-sensitive. This profile makes bears a model for sarcopenia, disuse osteoporosis, chronic kidney disease, and diabetes. Liu et al. (Cell Reports 2014) identified polar-bear-specific mutations in APOB (lipid metabolism) that may underlie their ability to subsist on a >30% fat diet without atherosclerosis.

citations.label: Liu et al. 2014, Cell — Polar bear genome & APOB
Nelson et al. 2014, Int J Obes — Bear hibernation physiology

Acomys cahirinus.headline: Only known mammal that regenerates skin, hair follicles, cartilage, and muscle without scarring.

regeneration.summary: Seifert et al. (Nature 2012) showed Acomys can autotomize skin to escape predators and subsequently regenerate the tissue — including hair follicles, sebaceous glands, dermis, and cartilage — with minimal scarring. This is unique among mammals. The regenerative response involves blastema-like structures and ECM composition more similar to salamanders than to Mus. Acomys has become a key comparative model for scar-free wound healing.

citations.label: Seifert et al. 2012, Nature — Skin regeneration in Acomys
Gawriluk et al. 2016, Nat Commun — Comparative regeneration

Fukomys damarensis.headline: Second eusocial mammal; shares longevity and cancer resistance with its cousin the naked mole-rat.

longevity.summary: Damaraland mole-rats live ~20 years, far longer than similarly sized rodents, and are the only other known eusocial mammal besides Heterocephalus glaber. They provide a comparative reference for separating NMR-specific mechanisms from convergent bathyergid adaptations. Their longevity is associated with suppressed mTOR signaling and enhanced oxidative stress defenses.

citations.label: Dammann 2017, Biogerontology — Long-lived mole-rats as aging models

Rousettus aegyptiacus.headline: Natural reservoir for Marburg virus without disease — a model for immune tolerance.

viral_tolerance.summary: Rousettus aegyptiacus is the confirmed natural reservoir of Marburg virus. Guito et al. (Current Biology 2021) showed asymptomatic Marburg infection in this species is mediated by 'immunoprotective disease tolerance' — the bats clear the virus without the hyperinflammation that drives hemorrhagic disease in humans. Transcriptomic signatures show tempered IFN responses and downregulated inflammatory cytokines.

citations.label: Guito et al. 2021, Curr Biol — Marburg disease tolerance

Marmota marmota.headline: Hibernates for 6 months annually with reversible insulin resistance and protected cardiac function.

hibernation.summary: Alpine marmots undergo multi-day torpor bouts interspersed with brief arousals. Their cardiac conduction is protected against hypothermia-induced arrhythmias — a property being studied for deep hypothermic circulatory arrest in cardiac surgery. Connexin 43 and 45 remodeling during torpor appears key.

citations.label: Fedorov et al. 2009, Physiol Genomics — Hibernating cardiac conduction

Homo sapiens.headline: Reference species — target of the therapeutic translation.

Mus musculus.headline: Standard lab model; short lifespan and high cancer incidence make it a useful negative control.

Rattus norvegicus.headline: Standard lab model; included as a phylogenetic reference.

Hydrochoerus hydrochaeris.headline: Largest living rodent — phylogenetic context for bathyergid comparisons.

Giraffa tippelskirchi.headline: Blood pressure of ~280/180 mmHg without hypertensive organ damage — a cardiovascular outlier.

innate_immunity.summary: Giraffes maintain the highest systemic blood pressure of any mammal without developing the left ventricular hypertrophy, renal damage, or retinopathy seen in hypertensive humans. Agaba et al. (Nat Commun 2016) sequenced the giraffe genome and identified positively selected genes in FGFRL1 (cardiovascular development), and fibrosis pathways. The giraffe kidney handles supranormal perfusion pressure via thickened basement membranes and altered RAAS signaling.

citations.label: Agaba et al. 2016, Nat Commun — Giraffe genome

Daubentonia madagascariensis.headline: Phylogenetic placeholder — illustrates primate diversity on the tree.

Enhydra lutris.headline: Highest mass-specific metabolic rate of any marine mammal — thermogenesis model.

Canis lupus familiaris.headline: Comparative oncology partner — naturally occurring tumors that parallel human cancers.

Macaca mulatta.headline: Closest clinically used non-human primate model.

Pan troglodytes.headline: Closest living relative to humans; useful reference for evolutionary comparisons.

Myotis davidii.headline: Source genome for the NLRP3 splice-variant discovery in bats.

viral_tolerance.summary: Along with P. alecto, M. davidii was one of the two species in which the NLRP3 exon-7-negative splice variant was first identified, and contributed to establishing the bat-specific dampened inflammasome phenotype.

citations.label: Ahn et al. 2019, Nat Microbiol

Eptesicus fuscus.headline: TNFα c-Rel binding motif silences inflammation — promising autoimmune lead.

innate_immunity.summary: Banerjee et al. (Sci Rep 2017) identified a unique c-Rel repressor binding motif in the promoter of E. fuscus TNFα that restricts TNFα transcription even under strong innate-immune stimulation. This is a rare example of a specific cis-regulatory change with clear therapeutic implications for anti-TNF biology in autoimmune disease.

citations.label: Banerjee et al. 2017, Sci Rep — c-Rel silencing of bat TNFα

Tursiops truncatus.headline: Extreme diving physiology with elevated myoglobin and selective vasoconstriction.

hypoxia_tolerance.summary: Like other cetaceans, bottlenose dolphins carry high-charge myoglobin that allows extreme concentrations without aggregation. They share the convergent deep-diving adaptation profile described by Mirceta et al.

citations.label: Mirceta et al. 2013, Science

Rangifer tarandus.headline: Antler regrowth is the fastest known mammalian tissue regeneration — ~2 cm/day.

regeneration.summary: Deer antlers regenerate annually, representing the only complete, periodic regeneration of a mammalian appendage. Wang et al. (Science 2019) described the deer genome and antler stem-cell biology, identifying oncogene-like proliferation genes expressed in a controlled, tumor-free manner. Comparative analysis shows convergent activation of tumor suppressors that balance this proliferation.

citations.label: Wang et al. 2019, Science — Ruminant genomes & antler regeneration

Choloepus hoffmanni.headline: Extreme metabolic economy — phylogenetic reference within Xenarthra.

Dasypus novemcinctus.headline: Only non-human natural host of Mycobacterium leprae — leprosy model.

Sorex araneus.headline: Seasonal skull shrinkage (Dehnel's phenomenon) — brain regenerative plasticity.

Trichechus manatus.headline: Afrotherian phylogenetic reference; shares ancestry with elephants.

Orycteropus afer.headline: Sole extant tubulidentate — broadens afrotherian coverage of the tree.
